content.heading: Return Values — making your function answer a question

blocks.alt: A function is like a machine. It takes in inputs, does something with them, and gives back an output. A function that only logs shows something on screen but hands nothing back. The caller gets undefined.
blocks.caption: A function is like a machine. It takes in inputs, does something with them, and gives back an output. A function that only logs shows something on screen but hands nothing back. The caller gets undefined.

blocks.text: A function can do two things: it can log output so a human can read it, or it can return a value so the rest of your code can use it. The return keyword sends a value back to whoever called the function — and the function stops the moment it hits return.

blocks.desc: Send this value back to the caller — the function stops here
Call the function and capture whatever it returns into a variable

blocks.text: Here are two versions of the same function. The first only logs — the result is printed but lost. The second uses return, so the caller gets the value back and can keep using it.

lines: // This function only logs — result disappears after it prints
// This function returns — the caller gets the value back
let answer = doubleReturn(5);   // answer is now 10
console.log('Triple is:', answer + 5);  // we can keep using it

blocks.label: return vs console.log
blocks.text: console.log shows a value on screen — but that value is gone the moment it prints. return hands the value back to your code so you can store it, do math with it, or pass it somewhere else. A function that only logs cannot be used in math or stored in a variable.
The moment JavaScript hits a return statement, the function stops. Any code after it in the same function is completely ignored.

blocks.alt: The moment JavaScript hits a return statement, the function stops. Any code after it in the same function is completely ignored.
blocks.caption: The moment JavaScript hits a return statement, the function stops. Any code after it in the same function is completely ignored.

blocks.label: Think of it like an exit door
blocks.text: A function is a room. return is the exit door. The moment your code walks through it, it leaves the room. Anything left in the room stays behind.
This is why having return inside an if/else works so cleanly — only one branch ever runs, so only one return is ever reached.

challenges.title: The function isn't sharing its answer
challenges.description: The function addTen should give the caller back a number they can use. Right now the caller gets undefined. Fix it.
challenges.hint: console.log shows the value but does not send it back. Replace it with return.
challenges.explanation: console.log prints the value to the screen but the function still returns undefined. The caller stores that undefined in answer. Fix: replace console.log(result) with return result. Now addTen(5) hands back 15 and the caller can use it.

challenges.title: The score multiplier is broken
challenges.description: A game multiplies the score by a bonus and then adds 100 to reward the player. The final total is always 100 no matter what. Find why and fix it.
challenges.hint: What is boosted if the function never returns anything? What does undefined + 100 give you?
challenges.explanation: multiplyScore logs but doesn't return, so boosted is undefined. undefined + 100 gives NaN in most cases — but the point is the function's result is lost the moment it prints. Fix: return score * bonus. Now boosted holds 150 and total is 250.

challenges.title: The chain is broken
challenges.description: This code builds a greeting in two steps — first building the name, then wrapping it in a message. The final output should be 'Welcome to the game, Alex Smith!' but it prints something unexpected. There are two bugs, both related to return.
challenges.hint: Trace what name actually contains before it gets passed to buildGreeting. What does buildName hand back?

checks.includes: Welcome to the game, Alex Smith!
checks.label: logs the correct greeting

challenges.explanation: buildName logs the full name but returns undefined. So name holds undefined, and buildGreeting receives undefined, producing 'Welcome to the game, undefined!'. Fix: add return full inside buildName. When functions need to pass values to each other, every step in the chain must return.

exercises.title: Write functions that return
tasks: Write a function called multiply that takes two numbers a and b and returns their product.
Store the result of calling multiply(3, 4) in a variable called product and log it.
Write a function called fullName that takes a first and last parameter and returns the combined name as one string with a space in between.
Call fullName with your own first and last name, store the result, and log it.
Call multiply three more times with different numbers and log each result.
exercises.hint: Use the return keyword inside the function body. The caller needs to store the result in a variable to use it: let result = multiply(3, 4);

exercises.title: What does the caller get?
tasks: Before running: for each function below, write down what you think the caller gets back.
Write your predictions next to each console.log.
Run it and compare. Pay close attention to any 'undefined' that appears.
Explain in one sentence why greetLog and greetReturn behave differently for the caller.
exercises.hint: A function that only logs shows something on screen but hands nothing back. The caller gets undefined.

exercises.title: Fix the missing return
tasks: Read the code carefully. The function double is supposed to give back a value, but something is wrong.
Predict: what will tripled log, and why?
Run it and read the output. Was your prediction right?
Fix the function so that calling double(5) returns 10, and tripled logs the correct number.
exercises.hint: console.log inside a function shows output but does not send anything back. The caller gets undefined instead. Replace the console.log with a return statement.
